summaryZh:
- **基础设施与芯片**：AI-RAN讨论热、落地冷，产业链仍缺明确商业闭环与保障机制。  
- 英伟达被再度解读为“AI帝国”核心，算力、生态与资本优势持续放大。  
- 法国Mistral以8.3亿美元债务融资，推进巴黎附近数据中心建设，欧洲本土算力自建提速。  
- 泄露信息称Anthropic“**Mythos**”面向网络安全场景，AI模型垂直化与防务化趋势升温。  
- **AI助手 vs AI智能体**之争延伸至企业网络：自动驾驶网络推动运维自动化、降本增效。  
- 应用侧从“更快更便宜”转向“更大更好”，食品研发与营销等行业加速引入AI。  
- 马斯克“Chip-Vision”启动高薪招聘，折射顶级芯片人才竞争与高强度组织模式。

highlights.title: AI-RAN – lots of talk, little action, no guarantees - Light Reading
highlights.titleZh: AI-RAN——空谈很多，行动很少，没有任何保障——Light Reading
highlights.content: * [5G](/wireless/5g) * [AI & Machine Learning](/digital-transformation/ai-machine-learning) * [6G](/wireless/6g) * [Semiconductors](/network-technology/semiconductors) * [News](/latest-news) # AI-RAN – lots of talk, little action, no guarantees When it comes to Nvidia's GPUs, keeping AI separate from RAN compute might be the most sensible approach for most telcos. [Iain Morris,](/author/iain-morris)International Editor,Light Reading March 30, 2026 6 Min Read Nvidia's GPUs continue to be hyped, but it's still tumbleweed in AI-RAN town.(Source: Nvidia) Thieves who steal copper wires from telecom networks have plagued operators for years. A graphics processing unit (GPU), one of the pricey chips made by Nvidia, could be another juicy target for criminals in the radio access network (RAN), fears Bruno Zerbib, the chief technology officer of Orange. Nvidia's most powerful GPUs each sell for tens of thousands of dollars, helping to explain why its gross profit margins are still north of 70%. At the base of a cell site, or in a nearby, unguarded small data center, they may look irresistible to the average crook. Prospective GPU snatchers might have to wait a few years, though. Acres of coverage about the concept of AI-RAN, popularized by Nvidia, might have created the impression that GPUs are sprinkled liberally across the world's 7 million mobile sites. In fact, you are more likely to unearth a working Nokia phone, a functional relic of the 1990s mobile boom. Nokia, the only big RAN vendor designing software for GPUs, does not expect to have commercial products ready until the end of this year, confirmed Pallavi Mahajan, its chief technology officer (CTO), at Nokia's MWC press conference a few weeks ago. No telco has announced plans for a commercial deployment. All the noise is about trials and most of it emanates from just two operators – T-Mobile US and Japan's SoftBank. The availability gap was noted by John Saw, the former's chief technology officer, at the same press conference. "I would be really upset if a year from now Pallavi didn't deliver an AI-RAN radio," [he told analysts and reporters](/5g/t-mobile-eager-to-test-nokia-s-ai-ran-this-year), sounding more confident that the Seattle Seahawks will win the Superbowl again next year (they beat the New England Patriots 29-13 at this year's final on February 8). Nokia CEO Justin Hotard was quick to interject, promising that Saw will not be disappointed. "That's definitely going to happen," said Hotard (and not about the NFL). "That one I can assure you." But meeting his delivery target for an AI-RAN product does not mean T-Mobile will quickly embark on a major commercial rollout, especially when there is no compelling evidence that having a GPU-based RAN would give the operator a big advantage over its rivals in the battle for customers. ##### Slowly does it SoftBank, the other notable cheerleader for AI-RAN, has seemingly moved slowly and with caution despite some of the enthusiastic-sounding headlines. "We have been working on this development, and we are aiming to deploy a small number of sites maybe within the next fiscal year, within this year or maybe early next year. That is the plan," said Ryuji Wakikawa, the vice president of SoftBank's advanced technology division, just over a year ago. Supporting commercial traffic is not the same as running a trial, requiring a "different level of quality and functions," he told reporters on a call before MWC 2025. "Hopefully, a few sites within 12 to 18 months, and we will decide how to deploy further in our network after that," he said at the time. T-Mobile US and SoftBank are not the only big operators linked with GPU-based AI-RAN interest. A year ago, Indonesia's Indosat Ooredoo Hutchison (IOH) [was reported by Computer Weekly](https://www.computerweekly.com/news/366620460/Indosat-Ooredoo-Hutchison-claims-AI-RAN-first-for-Southeast-Asia), part of the same group as Light Reading, to have kicked off a "landmark" initiative to buil...

Silicon: A Deep Dive into Nvidia’s $4 Trillion AI Empire (2026) By: [Finterra](http://markets.chroniclejournal.com/chroniclejournal/news/provider/finterra) March 30, 2026 at 10:01 AM EDT *Date: March 30, 2026* ## Introduction As of early 2026, NVIDIA Corp. ([NASDAQ: NVDA](http://markets.chroniclejournal.com/chroniclejournal/quote?Symbol=NQ%3ANVDA)) has transcended its origins as a high-end graphics card manufacturer to become the undisputed architect of the global "Intelligence Economy." With a market capitalization fluctuating between $4.1 trillion and $4.4 trillion, Nvidia now rivals the GDP of major sovereign nations. This research feature explores how a single fabless semiconductor company achieved a valuation that dwarfs traditional manufacturing giants, driven by a relentless innovation cycle and a software-defined ecosystem that rivals the dominance of the internet's early protocols. ## Historical Background Founded in 1993 by Jensen Huang, Chris Malachowsky, and Curtis Priem, Nvidia initially focused on the niche market of 3D graphics for gaming. The company’s trajectory changed forever in 2006 with the launch of CUDA (Compute Unified Device Architecture). By allowing researchers to use GPUs for general-purpose mathematical calculations, Nvidia planted the seeds for the modern AI revolution. While the industry initially viewed CUDA as a distraction from gaming, it became the foundation for the Deep Learning breakthrough of 2012 (AlexNet) and the subsequent Generative AI explosion of 2023. highlights.content: ### Topics [Biotech & Health](/category/biotech-health/) [Cloud Computing](/tag/cloud-computing/) [Commerce](/category/commerce/) [Crypto](/category/cryptocurrency/) [Enterprise](/category/enterprise/) [EVs](/tag/evs/) [Fintech](/category/fintech/) [Fundraising](/category/fundraising/) [Gadgets](/category/gadgets/) [Gaming](/category/gaming/) [Google](/tag/google/) [Government & Policy](/category/government-policy/) [Hardware](/category/hardware/) [Instagram](/tag/instagram/) [Layoffs](/tag/layoffs/) [Media & Entertainment](/category/media-entertainment/) [Meta](/tag/meta/) [Microsoft](/tag/microsoft/) [Privacy](/category/privacy/) [Robotics](/category/robotics/) [Security](/category/security/) [Social](/category/social/) [Space](/category/space/) [Startups](/category/startups/) [TikTok](/tag/tiktok/) [Transportation](/category/transportation/) [Venture](/category/venture/) ### More from TechCrunch [Staff](/about-techcrunch/) [Events](/events/) [Startup Battlefield](/startup-battlefield/) [StrictlyVC](https://strictlyvc.com/) [Newsletters](/newsletters/) [Podcasts](/podcasts/) [Videos](/video/) [Partner Content](/sponsored/) [TechCrunch Brand Studio](/brand-studio/) [Crunchboard](https://www.crunchboard.com/) [Contact Us](/contact-us/) In Brief Posted: * [Ivan Mehta](https://techcrunch.com/author/ivan-mehta/) # Mistral AI raises $830M in debt to set up a data center near Paris French lab Mistral AI has raised $830 million in debt to build a new data center near Paris that will be powered by Nvidia chips, according to reports from [Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/frances-mistral-raises-830-million-debt-ai-data-centre-build-up-2026-03-30/) and [CNBC](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/30/mistral-ai-paris-data-center-cluster-debt-financing.html). Mistral first announced plans to build [a data center last year](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-02-10/mistral-ceo-plans-to-raise-more-funds-to-back-ai-data-center), when its CEO Arthur Mensch said it would explore different financing options in February 2025. It plans to complete building the data center in Bruyeres-le-Chatel and make it operational in the second quarter of 2026, Reuters reported on Monday. Mistral did not immediately return a request seeking confirmation. Last month, the company said it would invest [$1.4 billion in Sweden](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/11/mistral-ai-infrastructure-sweden.html) to build out AI infrastructure, including data centers. Mistral said it aims to deploy 200 megawatts of compute capacity across Europe by 2027. “Scaling our infrastructure in Europe is critical to empower our customers and to ensure AI innovation and autonomy remain at the heart of Europe. We will continue to invest in this area, given the surging and sustained demand from governments, enterprises, and research institutions seeking to build their own customized AI environment, rather than depend on third-party cloud providers,” Mensch said in a statement to CNBC. Mistral has raised over €2.8 billion ($3.1 billion) in funding to date from investors including General Catalyst, ASML, a16z, Lightspeed, and DST Global, according to data from Crunchbase. highlights.title: AKA Foods brings AI to product development - Food Business News
highlights.titleZh: AKA Foods将人工智能引入产品开发——Food Business News
highlights.content: [Skip To Content](#skip-target) [Subscribe for Free](https://sos.dragonforms.com/SOS108_FBN_profile?pk=HeaderLink) [Subscribe Now](/subscriptions) [Home](/) » AKA Foods brings AI to product development # AKA Foods brings AI to product development 03.30.2026 By [Will Newton](/authors/118-will-newton) [Add as a preferred source on Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=foodbusinessnews.net&location=article_header) AMSTELVEEN, THE NETHERLANDS — AKA Foods, a European-based food technology company, is integrating artificial intelligence (AI) into product development processes with the launch of its AI-powered platform, AKA Studios. The platform is intended to shorten the product development cycle by consolidating existing research and sensory data into an organized database, providing ease of access to the information. The platform also provides an AI agent that can aid developers in product research and reformulation, such as a shift to a clean label, ingredient reductions or elevated sensory elements. The platform also can make the product development cycle more affordable, according to the company. David Sack, founder and chief executive officer of AKA Foods, said the platform was launched after noticing how “in shambles” data organization was within food companies. “The common thing we’ve seen across (consumer product goods) companies that we’re speaking to, from biggest to smallest, is that the most sophisticated tool they’ve got is Microsoft Excel or PDFs that they’re using, or actual notebooks in their product development labs,” Sack said. Shahar Rosentraub, chief product officer for AKA Foods added that, when companies face a problem in product development stages, the solution is typically hidden within the company’s existing data but often is overlooked or difficult to track down. Another complication hindering product development is the exit of company executives that take the knowledge of past projects with them. In response to the issues, the platform provides an avenue to retain and keep track of past knowledge and efficiently blend it with current research and development. “The knowledge is hidden in so many different silos, so that companies often can’t get to it in time,” Rosentraub said. “The food industry is just now discovering what other industries already have known — that they’re not fast enough to keep up. Smaller companies don’t have the 300 years of background that larger companies do, and the only way to win is to use AI tools to give them solutions to keep up.” Rosentraub compared the food industry to the pharmaceutical industry, claiming that, although it took some time to fully integrate, AI is now cemented within the industry. He predicts that the food industry is on its way to fully adopting the technology. With the speed that AI offers, the food development timeline can be expedited exponentially, according to the company. The expedited food development timeline allows companies to keep up with an industry’s constantly changing trends. “The pains of the industry are regulations,” Sack said. “They are always changing, and you always need new iterations of a (stock-keeping unit) to meet regulations. These are problems AKA Studios can solve.” The AI-powered platform is secure and does not share data to train models, according to the company. Users of AKA Studios maintain full ownership of their data. While incorporating AI into the product development process has its perks, the company recognizes that AI-powered technology is not perfect. Rosentraub said AI sometimes produces “hallucinations,” or false data, so factchecking is mandatory. He added that AKA Studios can eliminate the hallucinations, especially as AI continues to improve. “We try to take the good things that ChatGPT and the likes can give, which is mostly how they look at data very quickly, how they connect between different dots and insights of data,” Rosentraub said. “Then, we try to compensate on the things that these types ...

highlights.title: AI Assistants vs. AI Agents: - Hospitality Net
highlights.titleZh: AI 助手 vs. AI 智能体：- Hospitality Net
highlights.content: Reading time: [Opinion Article](/opinion) [General Management](/general-management) # AI Assistants vs. AI Agents: ## What hoteliers need to know before building their first “Digital Employee” The article explains how AI agents differ from assistants by working independently to achieve goals, potentially giving smaller hotels competitive advantages over larger brands. By [Ira Vouk](/author/148002468/ira-vouk), Hospitality Technology and Revenue Management consultant, [Ira Vouk Hospitality 2.0 Consulting](/consultant/17015110/ira-vouk-hospitality-20-consulting) Photo byIra Vouk Hospitality 2.0 Consulting There’s a lot of noise right now around “AI agents.” And if you’ve been nodding along in meetings pretending you fully get the difference between an assistant and an agent… you’re not alone. So let’s simplify this in plain hospitality terms. Because this isn’t just semantics — it’s a fundamental shift in how work gets done in our industry. **99% of people using AI today are still using it as an assistant, tapping into maybe 1% of what AI can actually do.** There’s a massive gap between that and what’s possible with AI agents, and most of it is accessible today without special skills or heavy investment. The small group of people who have learned to integrate agents into their workflows (including yours truly) are already operating at a completely different level — often 10x–100x more productive than everyone else. Same tools. Completely different mindset. The real shift isn’t about using AI more. It’s about using it differently. ### What’s an AI Assistant? (most of us are using this type) An AI assistant is reactive. * You ask → it answers. * You prompt → it generates. * You guide → it follows. Think of tools like ChatGPT, Claude browser version, or Gemini as your very fast, very capable intern who writes emails, summarizes reports, drafts marketing copy, and helps you think — but still needs you to tell it what to do, guide each step, and review everything. It’s the intern that works at lightning speed… but still looks at you after every task like, “What next?” It’s helping you do the work. ### Now: What’s an AI Agent? (1% of humans are using this type) An AI agent is proactive and independent. * You define the goal → it figures out the steps. * You set the outcome → it executes the workflow. Instead of “Write me a sales email,” you say: “Build a pipeline of corporate group leads in San Diego, qualify them, draft outreach, and prepare follow-ups.” And it finds data, connects tools, runs sequences, iterates, and delivers results — without you micromanaging every step. * No “what next?” * No waiting for instructions * No hovering over its shoulder like a very stressed-out supervisor This is not an assistant. This is a junior (sometimes surprisingly competent) team. “This Sounds Expensive and Complicated” - you’ll say. It’s not. You don’t need a dev team, custom software, or a six-figure budget. This has already been productized and democratized by tools like Claude, Perplexity, Zapier, n8n, Manus, and a growing list of hospitality-specific startups. You can literally start today. Not “in six months after IT approves it.” Today. ### How to Build Your First “Agent” (In 10 Minutes) 1. Install Claude on your desktop, sign up for the Pro account ($20/month — less than what you probably spent on coffee this week). You can also try Perplexity Computer or Manus or OpenClaw. 2. Give it a goal: “Build a list of corporate group prospects in San Diego and draft outreach emails.” Ask it what information it needs from you. Let it structure the workflow, suggest tools, and generate outputs. And no, you don’t have to know what tools and skills to connect. It will suggest or just do it all for you. 3. Review the result. 4. If it’s good – automate or repeat. That’s it. You’ve just created your first digital employee. No onboarding packet required. 1. Then scale it. Create one agent for marketing, one for sales, and one for reporting. 2. Once you ...

highlights.title: Faster, cheaper is good – but bigger, better is best - Marketing Week
highlights.titleZh: Faster, cheaper is good – but bigger, better is best - Marketing Week
highlights.content: # Faster, cheaper is good – but bigger, better is best We need to shift the AI conversation from efficiency to effectiveness. To do what great marketing actually does – drive growth. By [Tom Roach](https://www.marketingweek.com/author/tom-roach/ "Posts by Tom Roach") * [X](https://x.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.marketingweek.com/tom-roach-bigger-better-best/&text=Faster, cheaper is good – but bigger, better is best) * [Facebook](https://facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https://www.marketingweek.com/tom-roach-bigger-better-best/) * [LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.marketingweek.com/tom-roach-bigger-better-best/) The marketing narrative around AI has been stuck in first gear for a while and it’s now time to move it on. From efficiency to effectiveness. From new tech, to new growth. Technological revolutions tend to follow a familiar pattern. Once a new technology becomes cheap and ubiquitous, people go beyond just doing existing things cheaper and faster, and start making money with it in new ways. Entirely new ideas, products and jobs, not just more efficient versions of what went before. In 1865, William Stanley Jevons observed that as the steam engine became more efficient at burning coal, the total consumption of coal didn’t drop but skyrocketed. Because coal was now cheaper and more productive, it became viable for a thousand new uses. This is the Jevons paradox: new technologies initially create huge efficiencies, but they don’t lead to less consumption, they lead to an explosion of new utility. Similarly, the Nobel Prize-winning work of Philippe Aghion and Peter Howitt on creative destruction tells us that sustained growth does not come from doing the same thing more cheaply. It comes from dismantling old, low-value activities to reallocate resources towards more innovation. ## Marketing’s growth imperative Marketers are already proving to be keen and early adopters of AI in comparison to other disciplines, [according to McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai-2024). And unlike most other functions, marketing is also one of the major growth levers businesses have. This means marketers have an even greater opportunity to use it for growth not just efficiency. In fact, it’s our responsibility to invent and adopt the new, productive uses to drive growth for our businesses and brands. AI is helping marketers cut costs whilst also making more assets, in other words it’s a useful tool to help them comply with the demand to ‘do more with less’. But at some point this will need to give way to a newfound confidence to use this technology to help them deliver on their primary imperative – to help their brands grow. Synthetic audiences and insight, GenAI content and creative production, AI-powered creative testing and evaluation. All are proving to be powerful ways to do existing tasks faster, cheaper and at unprecedented scale. But these examples are closer to being ‘faster horses’ than a totally new invention. GEO is emerging as a new and innovative discipline (albeit with its roots in SEO) that promises to enhance discovery, visibility and demand for brands, so could be an early example of AI enhancing overall brand growth (not just replacing one form of demand capture for another). It’s also leading to new and innovative marketing services, like the [“Share of Model” platform](https://www.marketingweek.com/tom-roach-share-of-model-ai-era/) I have written about previously. [Over half of marketers ‘using AI’ for campaign creative](https://www.marketingweek.com/marketers-ai-campaign-creative/)The Brandtech Group, which owns Jellyfish, sees major time and cost savings, as well as performance gains when they deploy their GenAI creative and content platform Pencil Pro for major enterprise brands. Full disclosure: I work there and use Pencil with my clients. But even stepping outside of that, the average improvements we’re seeing are undeniably impress...

highlights.title: Elon Musks "Chip-Vision" Rekrutierung startet: Jahresgehalt von 2,33 Millionen Yuan, 7×24-Stunden Bereitschaftsdienst - eu.36kr.com
highlights.titleZh: Elon Musks "Chip-Vision" Rekrutierung startet: Jahresgehalt von 2,33 Millionen Yuan, 7×24-Stunden Bereitschaftsdienst - eu.36kr.com
highlights.content: [Startseite](/de) Artikel # Elon Musks "Chip-Vision" Rekrutierung startet: Jahresgehalt von 2,33 Millionen Yuan, 7×24-Stunden Bereitschaftsdienst 量子位2026-03-30 08:01 Entspricht dem Klischee von Musk When Elon Musk announced his Terafab chip plan, the job advertisements were already quietly available on the Tesla website. Lithography engineers are being sought in California, and silicon engineers in Texas. In addition, a technical project manager is needed who has already worked on projects with capital amounts of over one billion dollars to lead the whole thing. The offered salary goes up to $338,000 per year, which is approximately 2.33 million yuan. However, compared to the ambitious goals of this plan, this salary level may not be very impressive - Elon Musk has set the goal of producing a computing power of one terawatt per year, which is approximately 50 times the current annual global AI computing power. SpaceX has directly defined the project as "the next step on the way to a galactic civilization" in an official announcement. The job requirements, as usual, correspond to the stereotypical ideas of Musk... **7×24** hours on - call service. ## **2.33 million yuan for chip engineers** From the distribution of the jobs, it can be seen that Tesla is really looking for people who are involved in chip development this time. In Palo Alto, a Module Process Engineer for lithography is being sought. Lithography is one of the most technologically demanding steps in chip manufacturing. In this process, the chip design is etched onto a silicon wafer using extreme ultraviolet light, with an accuracy at the molecular level. Tesla requires applicants to have at least 10 years of experience in the top - level development of semiconductors. The offered base salary ranges from $88,000 to $240,000 (approximately 600,000 to 1.52 million yuan). Another job offer in California is for a Process Integration Engineer, who will be responsible for building the manufacturing process of advanced logic chips. The upper limit of the base salary is set at $338,280 (approximately 2.338 million yuan). In Austin, Texas, silicon engineers and a technical project manager with experience in managing "capital projects over $100 million" are being sought. But it's not easy to earn this high salary. AI Agents.title: AI Assistants vs. AI Agents: - Hospitality Net
AI Agents.titleZh: AI 助手 vs. AI 智能体：- Hospitality Net
AI Agents.content: Reading time: [Opinion Article](/opinion) [General Management](/general-management) # AI Assistants vs. AI Agents: ## What hoteliers need to know before building their first “Digital Employee” The article explains how AI agents differ from assistants by working independently to achieve goals, potentially giving smaller hotels competitive advantages over larger brands. By [Ira Vouk](/author/148002468/ira-vouk), Hospitality Technology and Revenue Management consultant, [Ira Vouk Hospitality 2.0 Consulting](/consultant/17015110/ira-vouk-hospitality-20-consulting) Photo byIra Vouk Hospitality 2.0 Consulting There’s a lot of noise right now around “AI agents.” And if you’ve been nodding along in meetings pretending you fully get the difference between an assistant and an agent… you’re not alone. So let’s simplify this in plain hospitality terms. Because this isn’t just semantics — it’s a fundamental shift in how work gets done in our industry. **99% of people using AI today are still using it as an assistant, tapping into maybe 1% of what AI can actually do.** There’s a massive gap between that and what’s possible with AI agents, and most of it is accessible today without special skills or heavy investment. The small group of people who have learned to integrate agents into their workflows (including yours truly) are already operating at a completely different level — often 10x–100x more productive than everyone else. Same tools. Completely different mindset. The real shift isn’t about using AI more. It’s about using it differently. ### What’s an AI Assistant? (most of us are using this type) An AI assistant is reactive. * You ask → it answers. * You prompt → it generates. * You guide → it follows. Think of tools like ChatGPT, Claude browser version, or Gemini as your very fast, very capable intern who writes emails, summarizes reports, drafts marketing copy, and helps you think — but still needs you to tell it what to do, guide each step, and review everything. It’s the intern that works at lightning speed… but still looks at you after every task like, “What next?” It’s helping you do the work. ### Now: What’s an AI Agent? (1% of humans are using this type) An AI agent is proactive and independent. * You define the goal → it figures out the steps. * You set the outcome → it executes the workflow. Instead of “Write me a sales email,” you say: “Build a pipeline of corporate group leads in San Diego, qualify them, draft outreach, and prepare follow-ups.” And it finds data, connects tools, runs sequences, iterates, and delivers results — without you micromanaging every step. * No “what next?” * No waiting for instructions * No hovering over its shoulder like a very stressed-out supervisor This is not an assistant. This is a junior (sometimes surprisingly competent) team. “This Sounds Expensive and Complicated” - you’ll say. It’s not. You don’t need a dev team, custom software, or a six-figure budget. This has already been productized and democratized by tools like Claude, Perplexity, Zapier, n8n, Manus, and a growing list of hospitality-specific startups. You can literally start today. Not “in six months after IT approves it.” Today. ### How to Build Your First “Agent” (In 10 Minutes) 1. Install Claude on your desktop, sign up for the Pro account ($20/month — less than what you probably spent on coffee this week). You can also try Perplexity Computer or Manus or OpenClaw. 2. Give it a goal: “Build a list of corporate group prospects in San Diego and draft outreach emails.” Ask it what information it needs from you. Let it structure the workflow, suggest tools, and generate outputs. And no, you don’t have to know what tools and skills to connect. It will suggest or just do it all for you. 3. Review the result. 4. If it’s good – automate or repeat. That’s it. You’ve just created your first digital employee. No onboarding packet required. 1. Then scale it. Create one agent for marketing, one for sales, and one for reporting. 2. Once you ...

AI Agents.content: * + [HOME](/ "Home") + [NEWS](/news-analysis "News") + [RESEARCH](/research "Research") + [LIVE DISCOURSE](/live-discourse "Livediscourse") + [BLOG / OPINION](/blogs "Write for us") + [SUBMIT PRESS RELEASE](/press-release "Press Release") * [About](/aboutus "About") * [Career](/career "Career") * [Advertisement](/advertisewithus "Advertisement") * [Team](/aboutus#devteam "Team") * [Partnership](/partners/knowledge-partners "Partnership") + [Knowledge Partnership](/partners/knowledge-partners) + [Media Partnership](/partners/media-partners) * [Contact Us](/aboutus#meet "Contact Us") * [NEWS](/news-analysis "News") * [RESEARCH](/research "Research") * [LIVE DISCOURSE](/live-discourse "Livediscourse") * [BLOG / OPINION](/blogs "Analysis / Blog / Opinion") * [INTERVIEW](/blogs/interview "Interview") * [SUBMIT PRESS RELEASE](/press-release "Press Release") * [Home](/) * [Blog](/blogs) * [Agro-Forestry](/news/agro-forestry) * Article # Why AI in Indian agriculture is stuck in pilot mode despite data boom --- [CO-EDP, VisionRI](/Home/AuthorArticles?fl=CO-EDP, VisionRI) | Updated: 30-03-2026 06:31 IST | Created: 30-03-2026 06:31 IST --- A new research paper suggests that the real challenge in scaling AI in agriculture is not technological capability, but the lack of robust, integrated, and usable data systems. The study finds that despite India generating vast amounts of agricultural data, structural weaknesses in data infrastructure are preventing AI from moving beyond pilot-stage applications. It highlights a critical disconnect between the availability of data and its usability for real-world AI deployment. Published as [*“Unlocking AI’s Potential in Agriculture: The Critical Role of Data”*,](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.23289) the paper provides a systematic assessment of India’s agricultural data ecosystem, examining national datasets, digital platforms, and implementation programs to identify why AI adoption in the sector remains limited. ### Data fragmentation, not algorithms, limits AI adoption in farming The study identifies a key paradox: while India has built one of the world’s largest digital public infrastructures, AI adoption in agriculture remains fragmented and largely experimental. This is in sharp contrast to sectors like healthcare and finance, where AI adoption rates have surged due to standardized and integrated data systems. According to the research, the primary constraint lies in the structure and quality of agricultural data. Data is generated across multiple institutions, including ministries, state agencies, and research bodies, but remains fragmented, inconsistent, and difficult to integrate. A key issue is the lack of interoperability. Soil data, weather information, crop statistics, and market data are often collected using different formats, timelines, and spatial references. Without common identifiers or standardized metadata, linking these datasets for AI-driven analysis becomes extremely difficult. Temporal misalignment further compounds the problem. Agricultural decisions often require real-time or near-real-time data, but many datasets are updated annually or seasonally. This mismatch reduces the relevance of AI models that depend on timely inputs. Spatial fragmentation is another major barrier. The absence of consistent geocoding systems means that datasets cannot be easily combined at the farm or plot level. As a result, AI systems struggle to deliver precise, localized recommendations, which are essential for smallholder farmers. These structural deficiencies, rather than limitations in algorithms, are the primary bottleneck to scaling AI in Indian agriculture. ### Governance gaps and access barriers deepen the challenge The research highlights governance and institutional barriers that restrict the effective use of agricultural data. Data ownership and access frameworks remain unclear, creating uncertainty for both farmers and technology developers. While legal protections for data privac...

The people building the future of artificial intelligence don't always agree, but when it comes to one deeply human question, their answers are consistent: Yes, you should still have kids. In a new documentary called "The AI Doc: Or How I Became an Apocaloptimist," directors Daniel Roher and Charlie Tyrell asked some of the most recognizable [CEOs in the AI race](https://www.businessinsider.com/ceos-predict-ai-bubble-losers-dario-amodei-larry-fink-2025-12) whether it is still safe to bring children into this world. "I think it's a great time to have a kid. We'll probably have another kid at some point," Anthropic's president and cofounder, [Daniela Amodei](https://www.businessinsider.com/anthropic-president-ai-humanities-majors-more-important-2026-2), tells the filmmakers. Amodei has one son, Galileo, according to a blog post from Stripe, where she worked for over five years. As major tech companies pour billions into building increasingly powerful systems that could one day surpass human intelligence, questions about the risks they may pose — especially to future generations — are prevalent. Researchers leading AI development have warned of [existential risks](https://www.businessinsider.com/ai-danger-doesnt-care-if-we-live-or-die-researcher-2025-9), including the possibility that advanced systems could pursue goals misaligned with human interests if left unchecked. The technology's impact on jobs is also a major concern for many parents. For [OpenAI CEO Sam Altman](https://www.businessinsider.com/sam-altman), the answer is also a yes. He said parenthood is "this momentous thing" that feels both exciting and overwhelming. "I stay up every night reading these books about how to raise a kid, and I hope I'm going to do a good job," he says in the film. He also said he's not worried about kids growing ...

LLMs.title: Robot vs. human - Axios
LLMs.titleZh: 机器人 vs. 人类 —— Axios
LLMs.content: [Skip to main content](#main-content) [Sign up for the newsletter](https://www.axios.com/signup/san-diego) 37 mins ago - [News](/local/san-diego/news) # Robot vs. human * [Joann Muller](https://www.axios.com/authors/jmuller) [Add Axios on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=axios.com) Add Axios as your preferred source to see more of our stories on Google. [Add Axios on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=axios.com) **A [Waymo robotaxi incident](https://www.axios.com/2026/01/29/waymo-accident-santa-monica-nhtsa)** outside a California elementary school in January suggests an AI brain would react faster than a human, but it's not that simple. **The big picture:** This incident fits into a much larger debate about whether autonomous vehicles can match — or exceed — the safety of human drivers. * People fear self-driving cars, yet nearly 40,000 people are killed each year in traffic accidents involving human drivers. * The answer to that question is crucial to winning the public's trust as robotaxis [spread quickly across America](https://www.axios.com/2025/11/28/robotaxis-waymo-may-zoox-tesla). **Catch up quick:** The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration is [investigating](https://www.nhtsa.gov/?nhtsaId=PE26001) after a Waymo robotaxi struck a child who ran across the street from behind a double-parked SUV. * Police said first responders evaluated the student, with her parent present, and did not report any injuries. **The intrigue:** Waymo claims its driverless vehicle behaved as expected, slamming the brakes as soon as it detected the child, slowing from 17 mph to under 6 mph before making contact. * A "fully attentive human driver" in the same situation would have hit the child at approximately 14 mph, according to Waymo's [computer modeling](https://waymo.com/blog/2022/09/benchmarking-av-safety). * "This significant reduction in impact speed and severity is a demonstration of the material safety benefit of the Waymo Driver, " the company wrote in a Jan. 28 [blog post](https://waymo.com/blog/2026/01/a-commitment-to-transparency-and-road-safety). **Reality check:** Reacting quickly isn't the only way to avoid crashes, safety experts tell Axios. Context, judgment and driving experience matter, too. * Young drivers have quicker reflexes, for example — but old drivers have much better safety records, notes AV safety expert Philip Koopman, emeritus professor at Carnegie Mellon University. * A careful, competent human driver would have avoided a panic stop in the first place by adjusting their driving behavior amid the chaos of school drop-off — or taking a different route altogether, he argued. *[Go deeper](https://www.axios.com/2026/02/01/ai-robotaxi-waymo-accident).* [Add Axios on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=axios.com) ## More San Diego stories [Politics](/local/san-diego/politics) ### [San Diego bases boost security after Iran strikes](/local/san-diego/2026/03/02/san-diego-military-bases-security-iran-strikes-naval-base-coronado-north-island) Mar 2, 2026 [Go deeper (1 min. read)](/local/san-diego/2026/03/02/san-diego-military-bases-security-iran-strikes-naval-base-coronado-north-island) [News](/local/san-diego/news) ### [San Diego-based USS Abraham Lincoln at center of Iran war strikes](/local/san-diego/2026/03/03/uss-abraham-lincoln-iran-strikes) Mar 3, 2026 [Go deeper](/local/san-diego/2026/03/03/uss-abraham-lincoln-iran-strikes) [News](/local/san-diego/news) ### [USPS stamps lowrider legacy in San Diego](/local/san-diego/2026/03/13/usps-stamps-lowrider-legacy-san-diego) Mar 13, 2026 [Go deeper (2 min. read)](/local/san-diego/2026/03/13/usps-stamps-lowrider-legacy-san-diego) [News](/local/san-diego/news) ### [SDSU misses March Madness, but UCSD is dancing](/local/san-diego/2026/03/16/sdsu-ucsd-basketball-march-madness-bracket-2026-ncaa-tournament) Mar 15, 2026 [Go deeper (1 min. read)](/local/san-diego/2026/03/16/sdsu-ucsd-basketball-march-madness-bracket-2026-ncaa-tournam...

LLMs.title: How AI helps achieve a best-in-class anti-fraud balance between security and service - FinTech Futures
LLMs.titleZh: AI如何助力在安全与服务之间实现一流的反欺诈平衡——FinTech Futures
LLMs.content: * [AI in Fintech](/fintech/ai-in-fintech) * [Cybersecurity](/regtech/cybersecurity) * [Financial Crime & Fraud Prevention](/regtech/financial-crime-fraud-prevention) * [Commentary](/latest-commentary) # How AI helps achieve a best-in-class anti-fraud balance between security and service Mitigating fraud while minimising friction is the holy grail of payments fraud prevention solutions. [Anna Milne,](/author/anna-milne) Editorial Contributor, FinTech Futures March 30, 2026 5 Min Read Sponsored by i2c After scooping the anti-fraud AI prize at the PayTech Awards USA 2025, Matt Pearce, VP of Fraud Risk Management at [i2c](https://www.i2cinc.com/) , tells FinTech Futures what the banking technology firm is most proud of about their solution, and how its use of AI in fighting fraud reflects the mounting challenge for financial institutions of all sizes. The [PayTech Awards USA](https://informaconnect.com/paytech-awards-usa/) , hosted by FinTech Futures , take place annually, celebrating the most innovative and impactful achievements in payments technology across the US. FinTech Futures: How are consumers and financial institutions increasingly affected by credit card fraud in the digital age? Matt Pearce: As digital payments expand, fraud is evolving alongside them. In our Consumer Credit Economy report on credit card fraud, we found about 28% of consumers experienced credit card fraud in the past year, and more than 70% say they’re at least somewhat worried about it.  Industry data reinforces the trend. The Nilson Report found more than 503,000 credit card fraud cases were reported in the US in the first three quarters of 2025 - that’s a 54% increase year over year. For financial institutions, the challenge is balancing speed with security. Payments now happen in real time, so fraud detection must keep pace. How can providers such as yourselves harness AI and machine learning (ML) to help combat fraud? Fraudsters move fast, so defences must move even faster. We use AI to evaluate risk directly within the transaction authorisation process, helping stop suspicious activity before funds are released, while maintaining approval rates. At the same time, machine learning models build behavioural profiles within 60–90 days, learning spending patterns and continuously evaluating transactions as new data arrives. i2c was recently awarded the Excellence in Tech: Best AI Solution – Fraud Fighter award at the PayTech Awards USA. What are you most proud of about your solution? We’re proud of the balance we have achieved between security and customer experience. Many fraud programs focus only on loss mitigation; we focus on stopping fraud while minimising friction. Transactions are monitored 24/7 in real time, using AI-informed fraud models, adaptive rules and automated alerts. In some deployments, we’ve captured up to 40% of fraud while maintaining customer friction around 0.5%, outperforming traditional benchmarks. Matt Pearce, VP of Fraud Risk Management at i2c Why do you think it won this award over other solutions - what sets it apart? Our fraud platform is embedded directly within the processing platform - it isn’t a bolt-on solution. Clients can configure controls easily, from withdrawal limits to overseas transactions or fraud rules. They also benefit from consortium intelligence drawn from fraud patterns across the global platform. We recently worked with a leading global payments network to increase authorisation rates in Latin America. Their feedback says it best: “You guys are the best-in-class. Not only are you beating the industry standards, you’re actually the best-in-class across the board.” What was the biggest driver to develop this solution, and what was the greatest challenge in building it? Financial institutions must balance fraud protection with customer experience. For more than 15 years, i2c has invested in AI, machine learning and data science to help institutions manage fraud as digital payments scale. The biggest chal...

LLMs.title: ASUS launches GeForce RTX 5080 PRIMO EVO series with vapor chamber missing - VideoCardz.com
LLMs.titleZh: ASUS launches GeForce RTX 5080 PRIMO EVO series with vapor chamber missing - VideoCardz.com
LLMs.content: * [News](https://videocardz.com/sections/news) * [Graphics](https://videocardz.com/topics/graphics) # ASUS launches GeForce RTX 5080 PRIME EVO series with vapor chamber missing Published: Mar 30th 2026, 10:37 GMT by WhyCry  [Comments](https://videocardz.com/newz/asus-launches-geforce-rtx-5080-primo-evo-series-with-vapor-chamber-missing#disqus_thread "Comments") [GeForce RTX 5080](https://videocardz.com/nvidia/geforce-50/geforce-rtx-5080) Latest [Send a tip!](https://videocardz.com/send-us-a-tip) [15:34 Mar 30 Fiber optic HDMI 2.1 cable enables 48 Gbps bandwidth over distances of up to 300 meters](https://videocardz.com/newz/fiber-optic-hdmi-2-1-cable-enables-48-gbps-bandwidth-over-distances-of-up-to-300-meters "Fiber optic HDMI 2.1 cable enables 48 Gbps bandwidth over distances of up to 300 meters")[14:09 Mar 30 Enthusiast gets Intel Core 9 273PQE “Bartlett Lake-S” boot on consumer Z790 board](https://videocardz.com/newz/enthusiast-gets-intel-core-9-273pqe-bartlett-lake-s-boot-on-consumer-z790-board "Enthusiast gets Intel Core 9 273PQE “Bartlett Lake-S” boot on consumer Z790 board")[12:58 Mar 30 No, GeForce RTX 60 specs did not leak (yet)](https://videocardz.com/newz/no-geforce-rtx-60-specs-did-not-leak-yet "No, GeForce RTX 60 specs did not leak (yet)")[12:37 Mar 30 ASUS launches GeForce RTX 5080 PRIME EVO series with vapor chamber missing](https://videocardz.com/newz/asus-launches-geforce-rtx-5080-primo-evo-series-with-vapor-chamber-missing "ASUS launches GeForce RTX 5080 PRIME EVO series with vapor chamber missing") ## ASUS PRIME RTX 5080 EVO launched ASUS has quietly added two GeForce RTX 5080 cards to its PRIME series with the EVO suffix. At first glance, it is not obvious what EVO means here. ASUS has used that label in different ways before, sometimes for revised coolers, different display outputs, or minor clock changes, so this one required a closer look. On paper, the PRIME RTX 5080 and PRIME RTX 5080 EVO look the same. ASUS lists the same dimensions, the same 2.5-slot design, the same display outputs, and the same feature set across the product pages. Even the clock speeds match on the OC models, which made it look like a simple naming update at first. Vapor Chamber is missing on PRIME EVO Series The difference appears to be hidden in the cooler design. ASUS mentions a vapor chamber in the image gallery on the original PRIME RTX 5080 page, but that reference does not appear on the EVO listing. ASUS does not call this out clearly in the spec sheet, so the change is easy to miss unless you go through the gallery images and model labels carefully. As you can see, the packaging doesn’t even mention EVO. However, if you look closely at the cooler, you will notice the vapor chamber is missing (main image above). As for the specs, both EVO cards still use 16GB of GDDR7 memory on a 256-bit bus at 30 Gbps. They keep one HDMI 2.1b and three DisplayPort 2.1b connectors, measure 304 × 126 × 50 mm, use a single 16-pin power connector, and carry an 850W PSU recommendation. The only listed difference between the two EVO models themselves is clock speed. The PRIME-RTX5080-O16G-EVO is rated at up to 2685 MHz in OC mode, while the PRIME-RTX5080-16G-EVO is rated at up to 2640 MHz. If you are shopping for a PRIME RTX 5080, you may need to check the exact model label rather than rely on the box design alone. This is sketchy move by ASUS for sure, usually they have no issue adding EVO to the box. LLMs.title: Bearman reacts to 50G crash during Japanese Grand Prix - F1
LLMs.titleZh: Bearman reacts to 50G crash during Japanese Grand Prix - F1
LLMs.content: [Skip to content](#maincontent) [(opens in a new tab)](https://www.f1authentics.com/?utm_source=f1&utm_medium=website_topnav&utm_campaign=2026_season)[(opens in a new tab)](https://f1store.formula1.com/en/?_s=bm-fi-f1-prtsite-topnav-230720-jm)[(opens in a new tab)](https://tickets.formula1.com)[Hospitality (opens in a new tab)](https://tickets.formula1.com/en/h-formula1-hospitality)[Experiences (opens in a new tab)](https://f1experiences.com/?utm_source=formula1.com&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=general) [(opens in a new tab)](https://f1tv.formula1.com)[Sign In](https://account.formula1.com/#/en/login?redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.formula1.com%2Fen%2Flatest%2Farticle%2Fbearman-reacts-to-50g-crash-during-japanese-grand-prix.7hiNVcluoZHH9AuiQGbQIO&lead_source=web_f1core)[Subscribe](/subscribe-to-f1-tv) [Oliver Bearman](/en/latest/tags/oliver-bearman.4DAvcXYHPgf115LKiHodRB)[Haas](/en/latest/tags/haas.5V60e311lYSIsWMe4OWA6M)[Franco Colapinto](/en/latest/tags/franco-colapinto.1hkfjh1TmDUA7rvBFGoKBh) # Bearman reacts to 50G crash during Japanese Grand Prix Ollie Bearman crashed heavily during the Japanese Grand Prix, with the Safety Car having to be deployed. Ollie Bearman has reflected on his high-speed crash during the Japanese Grand Prix, with the Haas driver involved in a 50G collision into the barriers. The 20-year-old suffered the accident approaching the mid-point of [Sunday's 53-lap race at the Suzuka Circuit](https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article/antonelli-takes-championship-lead-after-surging-to-victory-in-japan-from.4EC4uZc29IUEO2iE5nKpUp), having taken to the grass on the entry to the Spoon Curve after taking avoiding action because of a big closing speed to Franco Colapinto's Alpine in front. Having hit the barrier on the outside of the corner, [Bearman](/en/drivers/oliver-bearman) was able to extract himself from the car but was seen limping and being helped by marshals before being transferred to the circuit's Medical Centre where he was given the all-clear. "Everything is good, I'm absolutely fine," said Bearman, who had been progressing through the field after starting P18. "[It] was a really scary moment that happened out there but everything is okay, which is the main thing. "The car is a bit worse for wear but we have a month now to reset and come back. I can only apologise from the bottom of my heart to the team for that because it's a lot of work for them." When asked if it was just bruising he had sustained in the crash, Bearman replied: "Everything is fine. Adrenaline wearing off, it's going to be a long trip home but I'm absolutely fine. "The important thing is that everyone is in really high spirits. We have a month now to reset everything and come back stronger in Miami. That will definitely be the goal." The incident occurred when Bearman had to take avoiding action after closing on Colapinto at speed, with the Argentine driver moving towards the middle of the track and narrowing the space. The stewards noted the incident before deeming no further action was necessary, but Bearman gave his take on how it had all played out. "It was a massive overspeed, 50kph which is a part of these news regulations that I guess we have to get used to," he said. "But also I felt like I wasn't really given much space given the huge excess speed that I was carrying. "It's something that we talked about on Friday with the other drivers and the stewards that we need to be a bit more lenient, a bit more prepared because of these huge deltas in speed. "I think as a group we warned the FIA what can happen and this has been a really unfortunate result of a massive delta speed we've not seen before in F1 until these new regulations." ### FIA issue statement following Bearman's crash After the conclusion of the Grand Prix in Suzuka, the FIA issued the statement below regarding Bearman's collision. AI Product.titleZh: AKA Foods将人工智能引入产品开发——Food Business News
AI Product.content: [Skip To Content](#skip-target) [Subscribe for Free](https://sos.dragonforms.com/SOS108_FBN_profile?pk=HeaderLink) [Subscribe Now](/subscriptions) [Home](/) » AKA Foods brings AI to product development # AKA Foods brings AI to product development 03.30.2026 By [Will Newton](/authors/118-will-newton) [Add as a preferred source on Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=foodbusinessnews.net&location=article_header) AMSTELVEEN, THE NETHERLANDS — AKA Foods, a European-based food technology company, is integrating artificial intelligence (AI) into product development processes with the launch of its AI-powered platform, AKA Studios. The platform is intended to shorten the product development cycle by consolidating existing research and sensory data into an organized database, providing ease of access to the information. The platform also provides an AI agent that can aid developers in product research and reformulation, such as a shift to a clean label, ingredient reductions or elevated sensory elements. The platform also can make the product development cycle more affordable, according to the company. David Sack, founder and chief executive officer of AKA Foods, said the platform was launched after noticing how “in shambles” data organization was within food companies. “The common thing we’ve seen across (consumer product goods) companies that we’re speaking to, from biggest to smallest, is that the most sophisticated tool they’ve got is Microsoft Excel or PDFs that they’re using, or actual notebooks in their product development labs,” Sack said. Shahar Rosentraub, chief product officer for AKA Foods added that, when companies face a problem in product development stages, the solution is typically hidden within the company’s existing data but often is overlooked or difficult to track down. Another complication hindering product development is the exit of company executives that take the knowledge of past projects with them. In response to the issues, the platform provides an avenue to retain and keep track of past knowledge and efficiently blend it with current research and development. “The knowledge is hidden in so many different silos, so that companies often can’t get to it in time,” Rosentraub said. “The food industry is just now discovering what other industries already have known — that they’re not fast enough to keep up. Smaller companies don’t have the 300 years of background that larger companies do, and the only way to win is to use AI tools to give them solutions to keep up.” Rosentraub compared the food industry to the pharmaceutical industry, claiming that, although it took some time to fully integrate, AI is now cemented within the industry. He predicts that the food industry is on its way to fully adopting the technology. With the speed that AI offers, the food development timeline can be expedited exponentially, according to the company. The expedited food development timeline allows companies to keep up with an industry’s constantly changing trends. “The pains of the industry are regulations,” Sack said. “They are always changing, and you always need new iterations of a (stock-keeping unit) to meet regulations. These are problems AKA Studios can solve.” The AI-powered platform is secure and does not share data to train models, according to the company. Users of AKA Studios maintain full ownership of their data. While incorporating AI into the product development process has its perks, the company recognizes that AI-powered technology is not perfect. Rosentraub said AI sometimes produces “hallucinations,” or false data, so factchecking is mandatory. He added that AKA Studios can eliminate the hallucinations, especially as AI continues to improve. “We try to take the good things that ChatGPT and the likes can give, which is mostly how they look at data very quickly, how they connect between different dots and insights of data,” Rosentraub said. “Then, we try to compensate on the things that these types ...

AI Product.title: Faster, cheaper is good – but bigger, better is best - Marketing Week
AI Product.titleZh: Faster, cheaper is good – but bigger, better is best - Marketing Week
AI Product.content: # Faster, cheaper is good – but bigger, better is best We need to shift the AI conversation from efficiency to effectiveness. To do what great marketing actually does – drive growth. By [Tom Roach](https://www.marketingweek.com/author/tom-roach/ "Posts by Tom Roach") * [X](https://x.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.marketingweek.com/tom-roach-bigger-better-best/&text=Faster, cheaper is good – but bigger, better is best) * [Facebook](https://facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https://www.marketingweek.com/tom-roach-bigger-better-best/) * [LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.marketingweek.com/tom-roach-bigger-better-best/) The marketing narrative around AI has been stuck in first gear for a while and it’s now time to move it on. From efficiency to effectiveness. From new tech, to new growth. Technological revolutions tend to follow a familiar pattern. Once a new technology becomes cheap and ubiquitous, people go beyond just doing existing things cheaper and faster, and start making money with it in new ways. Entirely new ideas, products and jobs, not just more efficient versions of what went before. In 1865, William Stanley Jevons observed that as the steam engine became more efficient at burning coal, the total consumption of coal didn’t drop but skyrocketed. Because coal was now cheaper and more productive, it became viable for a thousand new uses. This is the Jevons paradox: new technologies initially create huge efficiencies, but they don’t lead to less consumption, they lead to an explosion of new utility. Similarly, the Nobel Prize-winning work of Philippe Aghion and Peter Howitt on creative destruction tells us that sustained growth does not come from doing the same thing more cheaply. It comes from dismantling old, low-value activities to reallocate resources towards more innovation. ## Marketing’s growth imperative Marketers are already proving to be keen and early adopters of AI in comparison to other disciplines, [according to McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai-2024). And unlike most other functions, marketing is also one of the major growth levers businesses have. This means marketers have an even greater opportunity to use it for growth not just efficiency. In fact, it’s our responsibility to invent and adopt the new, productive uses to drive growth for our businesses and brands. AI is helping marketers cut costs whilst also making more assets, in other words it’s a useful tool to help them comply with the demand to ‘do more with less’. But at some point this will need to give way to a newfound confidence to use this technology to help them deliver on their primary imperative – to help their brands grow. Synthetic audiences and insight, GenAI content and creative production, AI-powered creative testing and evaluation. All are proving to be powerful ways to do existing tasks faster, cheaper and at unprecedented scale. But these examples are closer to being ‘faster horses’ than a totally new invention. GEO is emerging as a new and innovative discipline (albeit with its roots in SEO) that promises to enhance discovery, visibility and demand for brands, so could be an early example of AI enhancing overall brand growth (not just replacing one form of demand capture for another). It’s also leading to new and innovative marketing services, like the [“Share of Model” platform](https://www.marketingweek.com/tom-roach-share-of-model-ai-era/) I have written about previously. [Over half of marketers ‘using AI’ for campaign creative](https://www.marketingweek.com/marketers-ai-campaign-creative/)The Brandtech Group, which owns Jellyfish, sees major time and cost savings, as well as performance gains when they deploy their GenAI creative and content platform Pencil Pro for major enterprise brands. Full disclosure: I work there and use Pencil with my clients. But even stepping outside of that, the average improvements we’re seeing are undeniably impress...

automated Gestational Age (GA) Tool integrated directly into its handheld, semiconductor-based ultrasound devices. It is the first FDA-cleared “blind-sweep” ultrasound AI tool for estimating gestational age. * **The Efficacy & Scale:** The tool is proven to deliver results equivalent to a trained sonographer for patients between 16 and 37 weeks. Backed by the Gates Foundation, the tool is already deployed in Malawi and Uganda, with FDA clearance now paving the way for massive U.S. rural expansion. **How Butterfly Network is Automating the Obstetric Ultrasound** In modern medicine, the ultrasound is arguably the most critical diagnostic tool that is still fundamentally broken by its own operational requirements. Traditional ultrasounds are highly operator-dependent. You cannot simply hand a wand to an ER nurse or a rural health clinic worker and expect them to accurately measure a fetal femur length to determine gestational age; it requires years of specialized sonography training. This personnel bottleneck has created a global crisis. In the U.S., maternal care deserts are expanding rapidly, with nearly half of rural counties lacking obstetric services. Globally, the lack of basic prenatal imaging contributes to the 92% of maternal and neonatal deaths that occur in low- and middle-income countries. The company has received FDA clearance for a fully automated Gestational Age (GA) Tool. AI Product.title: How AI helps achieve a best-in-class anti-fraud balance between security and service - FinTech Futures
AI Product.titleZh: How AI helps achieve a best-in-class anti-fraud balance between security and service - FinTech Futures
AI Product.content: * [AI in Fintech](/fintech/ai-in-fintech) * [Cybersecurity](/regtech/cybersecurity) * [Financial Crime & Fraud Prevention](/regtech/financial-crime-fraud-prevention) * [Commentary](/latest-commentary) # How AI helps achieve a best-in-class anti-fraud balance between security and service Mitigating fraud while minimising friction is the holy grail of payments fraud prevention solutions. [Anna Milne,](/author/anna-milne) Editorial Contributor, FinTech Futures March 30, 2026 5 Min Read Sponsored by i2c After scooping the anti-fraud AI prize at the PayTech Awards USA 2025, Matt Pearce, VP of Fraud Risk Management at [i2c](https://www.i2cinc.com/) , tells FinTech Futures what the banking technology firm is most proud of about their solution, and how its use of AI in fighting fraud reflects the mounting challenge for financial institutions of all sizes. The [PayTech Awards USA](https://informaconnect.com/paytech-awards-usa/) , hosted by FinTech Futures , take place annually, celebrating the most innovative and impactful achievements in payments technology across the US. FinTech Futures: How are consumers and financial institutions increasingly affected by credit card fraud in the digital age? Matt Pearce: As digital payments expand, fraud is evolving alongside them. In our Consumer Credit Economy report on credit card fraud, we found about 28% of consumers experienced credit card fraud in the past year, and more than 70% say they’re at least somewhat worried about it.  Industry data reinforces the trend. The Nilson Report found more than 503,000 credit card fraud cases were reported in the US in the first three quarters of 2025 - that’s a 54% increase year over year. For financial institutions, the challenge is balancing speed with security. Payments now happen in real time, so fraud detection must keep pace. How can providers such as yourselves harness AI and machine learning (ML) to help combat fraud? Fraudsters move fast, so defences must move even faster. We use AI to evaluate risk directly within the transaction authorisation process, helping stop suspicious activity before funds are released, while maintaining approval rates. At the same time, machine learning models build behavioural profiles within 60–90 days, learning spending patterns and continuously evaluating transactions as new data arrives. i2c was recently awarded the Excellence in Tech: Best AI Solution – Fraud Fighter award at the PayTech Awards USA. What are you most proud of about your solution? We’re proud of the balance we have achieved between security and customer experience. Many fraud programs focus only on loss mitigation; we focus on stopping fraud while minimising friction. Transactions are monitored 24/7 in real time, using AI-informed fraud models, adaptive rules and automated alerts. In some deployments, we’ve captured up to 40% of fraud while maintaining customer friction around 0.5%, outperforming traditional benchmarks. Matt Pearce, VP of Fraud Risk Management at i2c Why do you think it won this award over other solutions - what sets it apart? Our fraud platform is embedded directly within the processing platform - it isn’t a bolt-on solution. Clients can configure controls easily, from withdrawal limits to overseas transactions or fraud rules. They also benefit from consortium intelligence drawn from fraud patterns across the global platform. We recently worked with a leading global payments network to increase authorisation rates in Latin America. Their feedback says it best: “You guys are the best-in-class. Not only are you beating the industry standards, you’re actually the best-in-class across the board.” What was the biggest driver to develop this solution, and what was the greatest challenge in building it? Financial institutions must balance fraud protection with customer experience. For more than 15 years, i2c has invested in AI, machine learning and data science to help institutions manage fraud as digital payments scale. The biggest chal...

The tool, which let users upload their own faces into generated videos, had sparked suspicion online after it was discontinued only six months after launch. [Reports](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS8yMDI2LzAzLzI5L3doeS1vcGVuYWktcmVhbGx5LXNodXQtZG93bi1zb3JhLw%3D%3D&lang=en) Techcrunch.com. According to a Wall Street Journal investigation cited by TechCrunch, Sora’s biggest problem was weak demand combined with very high operating costs. The app reportedly reached about one million users at its peak, but that number later fell below 500,000. At the same time, keeping the service running was said to cost OpenAI around $1 million per day because AI video generation requires heavy computing power. That pressure appears to have forced OpenAI to rethink its priorities. While teams were focused on Sora, rivals such as Anthropic were reportedly gaining ground in areas more closely tied to long-term revenue, especially with products aimed at software engineers and business clients. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman ultimately chose to shut Sora down, free up computing capacity and redirect efforts elsewhere. The move appears to have been abrupt: the Wall Street Journal reported that Disney, which had committed $1 billion to a partnership, learned about the closure less than an hour before the public announcement, and the agreement ended alongside the product. automated Gestational Age (GA) Tool integrated directly into its handheld, semiconductor-based ultrasound devices. It is the first FDA-cleared “blind-sweep” ultrasound AI tool for estimating gestational age. * **The Efficacy & Scale:** The tool is proven to deliver results equivalent to a trained sonographer for patients between 16 and 37 weeks. Backed by the Gates Foundation, the tool is already deployed in Malawi and Uganda, with FDA clearance now paving the way for massive U.S. rural expansion. **How Butterfly Network is Automating the Obstetric Ultrasound** In modern medicine, the ultrasound is arguably the most critical diagnostic tool that is still fundamentally broken by its own operational requirements. Traditional ultrasounds are highly operator-dependent. You cannot simply hand a wand to an ER nurse or a rural health clinic worker and expect them to accurately measure a fetal femur length to determine gestational age; it requires years of specialized sonography training. This personnel bottleneck has created a global crisis. In the U.S., maternal care deserts are expanding rapidly, with nearly half of rural counties lacking obstetric services. Globally, the lack of basic prenatal imaging contributes to the 92% of maternal and neonatal deaths that occur in low- and middle-income countries. Today, [Butterfly Network](https://www.butterflynetwork.com/) announced a regulatory milestone that effectively bypasses this bottleneck. The company has received FDA clearance for a fully automated Gestational Age (GA) Tool. By introducing the first-ever “blind-sweep” AI to the market, Butterfly isn’t just upgrading the ultrasound; they are completely de-skilling it. *“Improving maternal health outcomes and expanding access to prenatal imaging has become an urgent priority, and Butterfly is proud to be the first to bring this type of technolog...

Open Source AI.title: How AI helps achieve a best-in-class anti-fraud balance between security and service - FinTech Futures
Open Source AI.titleZh: How AI helps achieve a best-in-class anti-fraud balance between security and service - FinTech Futures
Open Source AI.content: * [AI in Fintech](/fintech/ai-in-fintech) * [Cybersecurity](/regtech/cybersecurity) * [Financial Crime & Fraud Prevention](/regtech/financial-crime-fraud-prevention) * [Commentary](/latest-commentary) # How AI helps achieve a best-in-class anti-fraud balance between security and service Mitigating fraud while minimising friction is the holy grail of payments fraud prevention solutions. [Anna Milne,](/author/anna-milne) Editorial Contributor, FinTech Futures March 30, 2026 5 Min Read Sponsored by i2c After scooping the anti-fraud AI prize at the PayTech Awards USA 2025, Matt Pearce, VP of Fraud Risk Management at [i2c](https://www.i2cinc.com/) , tells FinTech Futures what the banking technology firm is most proud of about their solution, and how its use of AI in fighting fraud reflects the mounting challenge for financial institutions of all sizes. The [PayTech Awards USA](https://informaconnect.com/paytech-awards-usa/) , hosted by FinTech Futures , take place annually, celebrating the most innovative and impactful achievements in payments technology across the US. FinTech Futures: How are consumers and financial institutions increasingly affected by credit card fraud in the digital age? Matt Pearce: As digital payments expand, fraud is evolving alongside them. In our Consumer Credit Economy report on credit card fraud, we found about 28% of consumers experienced credit card fraud in the past year, and more than 70% say they’re at least somewhat worried about it.  Industry data reinforces the trend. The Nilson Report found more than 503,000 credit card fraud cases were reported in the US in the first three quarters of 2025 - that’s a 54% increase year over year. For financial institutions, the challenge is balancing speed with security. Payments now happen in real time, so fraud detection must keep pace. How can providers such as yourselves harness AI and machine learning (ML) to help combat fraud? Fraudsters move fast, so defences must move even faster. We use AI to evaluate risk directly within the transaction authorisation process, helping stop suspicious activity before funds are released, while maintaining approval rates. At the same time, machine learning models build behavioural profiles within 60–90 days, learning spending patterns and continuously evaluating transactions as new data arrives. i2c was recently awarded the Excellence in Tech: Best AI Solution – Fraud Fighter award at the PayTech Awards USA. What are you most proud of about your solution? We’re proud of the balance we have achieved between security and customer experience. Many fraud programs focus only on loss mitigation; we focus on stopping fraud while minimising friction. Transactions are monitored 24/7 in real time, using AI-informed fraud models, adaptive rules and automated alerts. In some deployments, we’ve captured up to 40% of fraud while maintaining customer friction around 0.5%, outperforming traditional benchmarks. Matt Pearce, VP of Fraud Risk Management at i2c Why do you think it won this award over other solutions - what sets it apart? Our fraud platform is embedded directly within the processing platform - it isn’t a bolt-on solution. Clients can configure controls easily, from withdrawal limits to overseas transactions or fraud rules. They also benefit from consortium intelligence drawn from fraud patterns across the global platform. We recently worked with a leading global payments network to increase authorisation rates in Latin America. Their feedback says it best: “You guys are the best-in-class. Not only are you beating the industry standards, you’re actually the best-in-class across the board.” What was the biggest driver to develop this solution, and what was the greatest challenge in building it? Financial institutions must balance fraud protection with customer experience. For more than 15 years, i2c has invested in AI, machine learning and data science to help institutions manage fraud as digital payments scale. The biggest chal...

The Nilson Report found more than 503,000 credit card fraud cases were reported in the US in the first three quarters of 2025 - that’s a 54% increase year over year. For financial institutions, the challenge is balancing speed with security. Payments now happen in real time, so fraud detection must keep pace. How can providers such as yourselves harness AI and machine learning (ML) to help combat fraud? Fraudsters move fast, so defences must move even faster. We use AI to evaluate risk directly within the transaction authorisation process, helping stop suspicious activity before funds are released, while maintaining approval rates. At the same time, machine learning models build behavioural profiles within 60–90 days, learning spending patterns and continuously evaluating transactions as new data arrives. i2c was recently awarded the Excellence in Tech: Best AI Solution – Fraud Fighter award at the PayTech Awards USA. What are you most proud of about your solution? We’re proud of the balance we have achieved between security and customer experience. Many fraud programs focus only on loss mitigation; we focus on stopping fraud while minimising friction. Transactions are monitored 24/7 in real time, using AI-informed fraud models, adaptive rules and automated alerts. In some deployments, we’ve captured up to 40% of fraud while maintaining customer friction around 0.5%, outperforming traditional benchmarks. Matt Pearce, VP of Fraud Risk Management at i2c Why do you think it won this award over other solutions - what sets it apart? Our fraud platform is embedded directly within the processing platform - it isn’t a bolt-on solution. Clients can configure controls easily, from withdrawal limits to overseas transactions or fraud rules. They also benefit from consortium intelligence drawn from fraud patterns across the global platform. We recently worked with a leading global payments network to increase authorisation rates in Latin America. Their feedback says it best: “You guys are the best-in-class. Not only are you beating the industry standards, you’re actually the best-in-class across the board.” What was the biggest driver to develop this solution, and what was the greatest challenge in building it? Infra & Chips.titleZh: AI-RAN——空谈很多，行动很少，没有任何保障——Light Reading
Infra & Chips.content: * [5G](/wireless/5g) * [AI & Machine Learning](/digital-transformation/ai-machine-learning) * [6G](/wireless/6g) * [Semiconductors](/network-technology/semiconductors) * [News](/latest-news) # AI-RAN – lots of talk, little action, no guarantees When it comes to Nvidia's GPUs, keeping AI separate from RAN compute might be the most sensible approach for most telcos. [Iain Morris,](/author/iain-morris)International Editor,Light Reading March 30, 2026 6 Min Read Nvidia's GPUs continue to be hyped, but it's still tumbleweed in AI-RAN town.(Source: Nvidia) Thieves who steal copper wires from telecom networks have plagued operators for years. A graphics processing unit (GPU), one of the pricey chips made by Nvidia, could be another juicy target for criminals in the radio access network (RAN), fears Bruno Zerbib, the chief technology officer of Orange. Nvidia's most powerful GPUs each sell for tens of thousands of dollars, helping to explain why its gross profit margins are still north of 70%. At the base of a cell site, or in a nearby, unguarded small data center, they may look irresistible to the average crook. Prospective GPU snatchers might have to wait a few years, though. Acres of coverage about the concept of AI-RAN, popularized by Nvidia, might have created the impression that GPUs are sprinkled liberally across the world's 7 million mobile sites. In fact, you are more likely to unearth a working Nokia phone, a functional relic of the 1990s mobile boom. Nokia, the only big RAN vendor designing software for GPUs, does not expect to have commercial products ready until the end of this year, confirmed Pallavi Mahajan, its chief technology officer (CTO), at Nokia's MWC press conference a few weeks ago. No telco has announced plans for a commercial deployment. All the noise is about trials and most of it emanates from just two operators – T-Mobile US and Japan's SoftBank. The availability gap was noted by John Saw, the former's chief technology officer, at the same press conference. "I would be really upset if a year from now Pallavi didn't deliver an AI-RAN radio," [he told analysts and reporters](/5g/t-mobile-eager-to-test-nokia-s-ai-ran-this-year), sounding more confident that the Seattle Seahawks will win the Superbowl again next year (they beat the New England Patriots 29-13 at this year's final on February 8). Nokia CEO Justin Hotard was quick to interject, promising that Saw will not be disappointed. "That's definitely going to happen," said Hotard (and not about the NFL). "That one I can assure you." But meeting his delivery target for an AI-RAN product does not mean T-Mobile will quickly embark on a major commercial rollout, especially when there is no compelling evidence that having a GPU-based RAN would give the operator a big advantage over its rivals in the battle for customers. ##### Slowly does it SoftBank, the other notable cheerleader for AI-RAN, has seemingly moved slowly and with caution despite some of the enthusiastic-sounding headlines. "We have been working on this development, and we are aiming to deploy a small number of sites maybe within the next fiscal year, within this year or maybe early next year. That is the plan," said Ryuji Wakikawa, the vice president of SoftBank's advanced technology division, just over a year ago. Supporting commercial traffic is not the same as running a trial, requiring a "different level of quality and functions," he told reporters on a call before MWC 2025. "Hopefully, a few sites within 12 to 18 months, and we will decide how to deploy further in our network after that," he said at the time. T-Mobile US and SoftBank are not the only big operators linked with GPU-based AI-RAN interest. A year ago, Indonesia's Indosat Ooredoo Hutchison (IOH) [was reported by Computer Weekly](https://www.computerweekly.com/news/366620460/Indosat-Ooredoo-Hutchison-claims-AI-RAN-first-for-Southeast-Asia), part of the same group as Light Reading, to have kicked off a "landmark" initiative to buil...

30, 2026* ## Introduction As of early 2026, NVIDIA Corp. ([NASDAQ: NVDA](http://markets.chroniclejournal.com/chroniclejournal/quote?Symbol=NQ%3ANVDA)) has transcended its origins as a high-end graphics card manufacturer to become the undisputed architect of the global "Intelligence Economy." With a market capitalization fluctuating between $4.1 trillion and $4.4 trillion, Nvidia now rivals the GDP of major sovereign nations. This research feature explores how a single fabless semiconductor company achieved a valuation that dwarfs traditional manufacturing giants, driven by a relentless innovation cycle and a software-defined ecosystem that rivals the dominance of the internet's early protocols. ## Historical Background Founded in 1993 by Jensen Huang, Chris Malachowsky, and Curtis Priem, Nvidia initially focused on the niche market of 3D graphics for gaming. The company’s trajectory changed forever in 2006 with the launch of CUDA (Compute Unified Device Architecture). By allowing researchers to use GPUs for general-purpose mathematical calculations, Nvidia planted the seeds for the modern AI revolution. While the industry initially viewed CUDA as a distraction from gaming, it became the foundation for the Deep Learning breakthrough of 2012 (AlexNet) and the subsequent Generative AI explosion of 2023.
